agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"CEO described a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services called DynaPricing. The description is high-level and intentionally vague — typical of an executive vision statement rather than a product spec. It establishes directional intent (demand-based pricing, automation, guardrails) but lacks specifics on thresholds, bounds, timing, and business constraints.","bounded_context":"Pricing","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"DynaPricing","definition":"The product/system that adjusts delivery prices in real time based on demand signals"},{"term":"Demand","definition":"A measure of current delivery request volume relative to capacity or baseline — exact definition unspecified"},{"term":"Guardrails","definition":"Upper and/or lower bounds or constraints on price adjustments to prevent extreme pricing outcomes"},{"term":"Real time","definition":"Price adjustments occur dynamically as demand changes, not on a batch/scheduled basis — exact latency requirements unspecified"},{"term":"Fully automated","definition":"The system adjusts prices without manual human intervention during normal operation"}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Delivery prices must increase when demand is high relative to some baseline or threshold","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy defining pricing behavior. The specific correlation between demand level and price increase is a tunable policy, not a system integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given demand exceeds the high-demand threshold, when a delivery price is calculated, then the price is higher than the base price"},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Delivery prices must decrease when demand is low relative to some baseline or threshold","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy defining pricing behavior. The inverse demand-price relationship at low demand is a tunable strategy, not a data integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given demand is below the low-demand threshold, when a delivery price is calculated, then the price is lower than the base price"},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Price adjustments must occur in real time — prices reflect current demand, not stale or batch-computed demand","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Real-time is a performance/freshness policy. The system would not be corrupt if pricing lagged by seconds; it would just be suboptimal. The acceptable staleness window is a business decision.","testable_assertion":"Given demand conditions change, when a new delivery price is requested, then the price reflects the most recent demand signal within the acceptable freshness window"},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"The system must operate fully automatically without requiring manual human intervention to adjust prices","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Automation is an operational policy. A human override or manual fallback does not corrupt system state — it is a design choice that could be relaxed.","testable_assertion":"Given the system is running in normal operating mode, when demand changes, then prices are adjusted without any manual human action"},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Guardrails must exist to constrain price adjustments within acceptable bounds","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Without guardrails, prices could become negative, zero, or astronomically high — any of which would represent a corrupt pricing state. The existence of bounds is a system integrity requirement even if the specific bound values are policy.","testable_assertion":"Given any demand signal (including extreme outliers), when a price is calculated, then the resulting price falls within the configured minimum and maximum bounds"},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"The pricing strategy must balance competitiveness with profitability — prices should not systematically price the company out of the market or below cost","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Competitive-yet-profitable is a strategic business objective, not a hard system constraint. The specific balance point is a business judgment call.","testable_assertion":"Given any demand condition, when a price is calculated, then the price is at or above the cost floor (profitability) and at or below the market ceiling (competitiveness)"}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"Demand is above the high-demand threshold","when":"A delivery price is calculated","then":"The price is higher than the base/normal price"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"Demand is below the low-demand threshold","when":"A delivery price is calculated","then":"The price is lower than the base/normal price"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"Demand is at an extreme level (very high or very low)","when":"A delivery price is calculated","then":"The price is clamped within the configured guardrail bounds (minimum and maximum)"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"Demand conditions change","when":"A new delivery is requested","then":"The quoted price reflects the current demand, not a previously cached or batch-computed value"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"The system is running in normal mode","when":"Demand fluctuates throughout the day","then":"Prices adjust automatically without manual operator intervention"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Demand drops to zero (no requests at all)","expected_behavior":"Price should hit the configured floor, not drop to zero or negative. Unclear if zero-demand even triggers pricing or if this is an idle state."},{"scenario":"Demand spikes instantaneously (e.g., major event or emergency)","expected_behavior":"Price must still respect the upper guardrail. The system must handle spike velocity without overshooting bounds."},{"scenario":"Demand oscillates rapidly around a threshold boundary","expected_behavior":"Prices should not flicker between high and low states. Some dampening or hysteresis mechanism may be needed to avoid customer confusion."},{"scenario":"Cost of delivery exceeds the guardrail maximum price","expected_behavior":"Unclear whether the system should price at a loss, refuse the delivery, or escalate. This is a critical unresolved scenario."},{"scenario":"Concurrent price calculations for the same zone at the same instant","expected_behavior":"All should return a consistent price — race conditions must not produce different prices for simultaneous requests in the same demand context."},{"scenario":"Demand data source becomes unavailable or returns stale data","expected_behavior":"Unclear whether system falls back to base price, last known price, or halts. Contradicts 'fully automated' if human intervention is needed."},{"scenario":"A customer sees one price but demand changes before they confirm","expected_behavior":"Unclear whether quoted price is honored or recalculated at confirmation time. Major UX and trust implication."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Dynamic pricing directly affects revenue and margins. Upside: capture surplus during high demand, stimulate volume during low demand. Downside: mispriced guardrails could erode margins or lose customers to competitors.","operational":"Fully automated system reduces manual pricing overhead but introduces operational complexity in monitoring, tuning demand signals, and managing guardrail configurations. Failure modes in automation could have outsized impact.","user":"Customers will experience variable pricing, which can erode trust if not communicated transparently. Price volatility may drive customers to competitors with stable pricing. Conversely, low-demand discounts could attract price-sensitive segments.","risk":"High risk of customer backlash if prices spike during emergencies or essential deliveries (surge pricing controversy, similar to ride-sharing). Regulatory risk in jurisdictions with price gouging laws. Reputational risk if guardrails fail publicly.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Will pay variable prices; may perceive unfairness during high-demand surges; may benefit during low-demand periods"},{"group":"Finance/Revenue team","impact":"Revenue becomes less predictable; need new forecasting models; margin protection depends on guardrail accuracy"},{"group":"Operations/Dispatch","impact":"Must provide reliable real-time demand signals; system failures directly affect pricing correctness"},{"group":"Couriers/Drivers","impact":"Unclear if dynamic pricing affects their compensation — if so, major labor relations impact"},{"group":"Competitors","impact":"May trigger pricing war or force industry-wide adoption of dynamic pricing"},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"Must ensure compliance with price gouging regulations, consumer protection laws, and pricing transparency requirements"}]},"confidence":{"level":30,"reasoning":"The CEO's description is a high-level vision statement with almost no specifics. There are no defined thresholds, no demand measurement methodology, no guardrail values, no pricing formula, no definition of 'real time' latency requirements, and no clarity on edge cases. The word 'probably' regarding guardrails further undermines confidence. Nearly every extracted rule required significant inference beyond what was explicitly stated.","high_confidence_areas":["Prices go up when demand is high (explicitly stated)","Prices go down when demand is low (explicitly stated)","System will be automated (explicitly stated)","Some form of guardrails will exist (stated, though hedged with 'probably')"],"low_confidence_areas":["What constitutes 'high' vs 'low' demand — no thresholds defined","What the guardrail bounds are (min/max prices) — not specified","How 'real time' is defined — sub-second? minutes? no SLA given","Whether demand is measured per zone, city, globally — geographic granularity unknown","Whether courier/driver pay is affected by dynamic pricing","How price quotes are honored if demand changes between quote and confirmation","Whether there is a manual override or kill switch despite 'fully automated'","What the pricing formula or curve shape is — linear, exponential, stepped","How the system behaves when demand data is unavailable","Whether 'competitive' means matching specific competitors or market average","Regulatory compliance approach for price gouging jurisdictions"],"assumptions":["There is a concept of a 'base price' that exists independent of dynamic adjustments","Demand can be quantified and measured in real time with sufficient reliability","Guardrails include both an upper bound (price ceiling) and lower bound (price floor)","The system applies to customer-facing delivery prices, not internal cost allocation","'Probably' regarding guardrails will be resolved to 'definitely' before implementation"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The CEO's description is a vision statement, not a spec. Nearly every operational detail is ambiguous: demand measurement methodology, threshold definitions, guardrail values, pricing formula, 'real time' SLA, geographic granularity, price quote honoring policy, and failure mode behavior are all unspecified. The word 'probably' on guardrails introduces ambiguity on whether they are even a firm requirement.","affected_stakeholders":["Product team","Engineering","Finance","Legal/Compliance","Customers"],"options":["Treat this as a discovery phase — conduct domain modeling workshops to resolve ambiguities before any implementation","Make assumptions on all ambiguous points, document them, and validate with stakeholders iteratively","Request a formal product requirements document from Product/CEO before proceeding"],"recommendation":"Do not begin implementation. Conduct structured domain discovery sessions to resolve at minimum: demand measurement definition, guardrail bounds, pricing formula shape, real-time SLA, price quote policy, and regulatory constraints. Each ambiguity here represents a potential production incident if resolved incorrectly by engineers guessing."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Competitive but also profitable' is a strategic tension without resolution criteria. Competitive could mean undercutting competitors, matching market rates, or maximizing volume. Profitable could mean per-delivery margin, overall margin, or contribution margin. These objectives can directly conflict and the spec provides no prioritization.","affected_stakeholders":["Finance","Product team","Executive leadership"],"options":["Define a minimum margin floor as the profitability constraint and let competitiveness float above it","Define competitor price matching as the ceiling and ensure costs stay below it","Create a multi-objective optimization with explicit weights for each goal"],"recommendation":"Establish a clear priority: profitability floor first (never price below cost + minimum margin), then compete within that constraint. This gives engineering a concrete invariant to implement."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Fully automated' conflicts with the practical need for human oversight, especially during edge cases like system failures, extreme events, or regulatory interventions. It is ambiguous whether 'fully automated' means no human can intervene, or simply that normal operation requires no human action.","affected_stakeholders":["Operations","Engineering","Legal/Compliance"],"options":["Fully automated with no human override capability","Fully automated in normal operation with manual override/kill switch for emergencies","Fully automated with human-approved guardrail configuration changes"],"recommendation":"Implement as automated-by-default with a manual override kill switch and audit trail. 'Fully automated' should mean normal operation, not the absence of emergency controls."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}
